context: 篇章词汇理解
question:
With the rapid progress in the economy, it is natural that people's ideology is bound for change. Being 26 is no longer a virtue to some people, and they are not alone in holding this view. Each might have different viewpoints on how to lead one's lifestyle: some prefer simplicity, some love luxuries, some spend 27, some accumulate as much as they can like a country mouse. As I was born poor and in the countryside, the simple rural life seldom loses its 28 to me, but as my station changes, I have an opportunity to 29 with the newly rich, an experience which has revolutionized my view of lifestyle.

It is evil or sinful to spend little and to save much, an idea only recently being revealed to me. Actually the whole world is in a

to borrow and to spend, with the USA leading the trend. The USA is still the richest and most powerful country in the world, but the

is that they borrow money from us. Though a

of economics, at least I know that investing with borrowed money will turn the borrower into the

rather than the decent and kind lender. An apparent case is real estate. Those who are

and have got loans from the bank become relatively rich by selling their purchased home at a price several folds higher. Even education is not immune from this theory. Many of those poor parents who have the vision to send their children to college have benefited from this investment, even though they have to

a living. It is reasonable to conclude that spending is praiseworthy, supposing it is not beyond your means.
explanation: 【解析】A）讲座中提到，与资源丰富的人相比，资源较少的人的意义和幸福感之间的关系更为密切。之所以会出现这种情况，可能是因为富裕的人有更多机会获得其他幸福来源。A)项中是对原文 have more access to other sources of happiness—many of which will be external 的同义转述，故答案为 A）。

context: 段落匹配
question: According to a senior researcher, we will have secure food supply if we rid ourselves of ignorance about native crops.
explanation: 27.【解析】B）空前是动词 spend，空后是逗号，分析句子可知，空格处可以填副词，修饰该动词；或者填名词，构成动宾结构。本句冒号后列举了人们不同的生活方式，其中 simplicity 与 luxuries 互为

context: 段落匹配
question: Most of the world's food supply comes from a tiny number out of thousands of crops that have been grown for centuries.
explanation: 28.【解析】A）空前的its与空格作动词loses的宾语，分析句子可知，空格处可以填名词。As引导原因状语从句，从句中作者提到：因为自己出身贫寒，生在农村。从句和主句的逻辑关系是：因为“我”出身贫寒，生在农村，所以朴素的农村生活对我总是有吸引力，备选的选项中符合语义的为A) appeal(吸引力)，同时与空后的to构成了固定搭配appeal to sb.“对某人的吸引力”。

context: 段落匹配
question: To provide their family with food when the staple crops failed, some African women farmers grew local crops in their backyards.
explanation: 31.【解析】D）空前的the与空格作后半句的主语，分析句子可知，空格处可以填名词。前半句提到，美国仍然是世界上最富有、最强大的国家，由转折连词but可知，后半句的语义与前半句形成对比关系。此句的语境为：虽然美国仍然是世界上最富有、最强大的国家，但具有讽刺意味的是，他们从我们这里借钱。备选的选项中符合语义的为D)irony(具有讽刺意味的事）。

context: 段落匹配
question: High-yielding crops have occupied a dominant position in modern agriculture since the green revolution in the last century.
explanation: 【解析】C）该段第1句提到，世界粮食供应的三分之二仅由四种作物提供；而第3句提到，有7000种作物人类已经种植了数千年。由此可知，题干中的Most of the world's food supply对应原文中的two-thirds of the world's food supply；题干中的thousands of crops对应原文中的7,000 crops；题干中的have been grown for centuries则对应原文中的have been farming for thousands of years。

context: 段落匹配
question: Growing alternative crops proved feasible in a variety of environments, but the critical question was whether they would be marketable.
explanation: 【解析】D）该段第3句提到，到2050年，世界必须多生产

context: 段落匹配
question: According to a professor, when the yields of staple crops fall, we will have to adapt to eating foods from alternative crops.
explanation: 【解析】B）文章前两段描绘了外星人看到人类在电影院看恐怖片的场景。第3段第1句表明观众处于高度兴奋状态，其中they指代的是上文中看电影的观众，即题干中的the audience in the movie theatre，B)项extreme excitement是对原文中high arousal的同义转述，故为答案。

context: 段落匹配
question: Urgent measures have to be taken to provide food for the projected world population without aggravating the climate.
explanation: 【解析】B）第6段提出到作者对许多人寻求恐怖娱乐这件奇怪的事感兴趣，第7段提到对这一问题的答案，第8段首句提到作者的研究表明人类在进化过程中，愿意在安全的环境中体验负面情绪，从而找到快乐。由此可知，寻求恐怖娱乐是为了在安全情境下模拟恐怖经历并从中获得快乐，其中find pleasure对应选项中的find joy，B)项是对该句的同义转述，故为答案。

context: 段落匹配
question: Colonial rule marginalised local crops by punishing Africans who grew them.
explanation: 【解析】A）第9段首句提到，这种模拟为儿童提供了关于如何避免成为猎物的重要信息，A)项是该句的同义转述，故为答案。

context: 段落匹配
question: As existing crops are endangered by global warming, we can increase food security by bringing back the many forgotten food crops.

context: Questions 46 to 50 are based on the following passage.
question: Why do many people seek out scary entertainment, according to the author's research?
options: A) They gain experience in overcoming horror in real life.
B) They find joy in going through simulated horrible experiences.
C) They have learned from hide-and-seek as kids the thrill involved.
D) They have evolved to gain pleasure in escaping life-threatening situations.
passage:
Imagine you're an alien sent to Earth to document the behaviour of the mammals inhabiting the planet. You stumble into a movie theatre that's showing the latest Hollywood horror film.

Several dozen humans are gathered together in a dark, undecorated room. They're all staring at a rectangular area on which patterns of light change rapidly.

They are clearly in a state of high arousal. Their heart rate is elevated, they occasionally glance around nervously, and they sometimes jump collectively in their seats, and emit high-pitched warning calls.

Eventually, the lights come up and the rectangular screen goes black. The humans stand up and leave the room, chatting and laughing, and showing signs of pleasure.

Why do these humans voluntarily expose themselves to what appears to be a deeply unpleasant experience? And why do they react so strongly to those patterns of light on a screen?

I am fascinated with the paradox of horror—the strange fact that many people seek out scary entertainment.

I think the answer to the puzzle lies in human nature.

My research suggests that we humans evolved to find pleasure in situations that allow us to experience negative emotions in a safe context. You can see these elements of horror in children's games. Take hide-and-seek for example, which is a simulation of a predator-prey interaction. The kid hides and the adult pretends to be a predator, searching for the child while howling like a dangerous beast.

This simulation gives the child crucial information about how to avoid becoming prey, and children tend to find that kind of activity deeply satisfying, presumably because it gives them a safe experience of a potentially catastrophic scenario.

They find it pleasurable, and pleasure is evolution's way of motivating us toward adaptive behaviour.

Horror is pleasurable to many people because it lets us play with negative emotions and develop coping strategies. We learn what it feels like to be truly afraid, and we learn how to handle negative emotion.

How, then, does horror work? My research suggests that horror works by exploiting an ancient set of biological defence mechanisms—an evolved fear system, which we share with other

animals. But humans are uniquely imaginative, and we use our evolved imagination to travel into virtual worlds that are full of danger.

There are good reasons for watching a horror film, even if you're not a loyal horror fan. If you make it through the film in one piece, you'll probably experience a strong sense of mastery, a sense that you were able to make it through an appalling experience. Anyway, watching a horror film makes you better at handling your own fear, and who knows when that will become critically relevant?

46. The alien finds the audience in the movie theatre clearly in a state of
explanation: 【解析】D）第1段第2句提到，但在数字领域，社交媒体决定了什么是美。由此可知，在数字领域，社交媒体定义美。D)项是该句的同义转述，故为答案。

context: Questions 46 to 50 are based on the following passage.
question: What do children learn from hide-and-seek?
options: A) How to avoid falling prey to an attacker.
B) How to simulate a predator-prey interaction.
C) How to keep themselves from catastrophic errors.
D) How to turn a dangerous scenario into a safe one.
passage:
Imagine you're an alien sent to Earth to document the behaviour of the mammals inhabiting the planet. You stumble into a movie theatre that's showing the latest Hollywood horror film.

Several dozen humans are gathered together in a dark, undecorated room. They're all staring at a rectangular area on which patterns of light change rapidly.

They are clearly in a state of high arousal. Their heart rate is elevated, they occasionally glance around nervously, and they sometimes jump collectively in their seats, and emit high-pitched warning calls.

Eventually, the lights come up and the rectangular screen goes black. The humans stand up and leave the room, chatting and laughing, and showing signs of pleasure.

Why do these humans voluntarily expose themselves to what appears to be a deeply unpleasant experience? And why do they react so strongly to those patterns of light on a screen?

I am fascinated with the paradox of horror—the strange fact that many people seek out scary entertainment.

I think the answer to the puzzle lies in human nature.

My research suggests that we humans evolved to find pleasure in situations that allow us to experience negative emotions in a safe context. You can see these elements of horror in children's games. Take hide-and-seek for example, which is a simulation of a predator-prey interaction. The kid hides and the adult pretends to be a predator, searching for the child while howling like a dangerous beast.

This simulation gives the child crucial information about how to avoid becoming prey, and children tend to find that kind of activity deeply satisfying, presumably because it gives them a safe experience of a potentially catastrophic scenario.

They find it pleasurable, and pleasure is evolution's way of motivating us toward adaptive behaviour.

Horror is pleasurable to many people because it lets us play with negative emotions and develop coping strategies. We learn what it feels like to be truly afraid, and we learn how to handle negative emotion.

How, then, does horror work? My research suggests that horror works by exploiting an ancient set of biological defence mechanisms—an evolved fear system, which we share with other

animals. But humans are uniquely imaginative, and we use our evolved imagination to travel into virtual worlds that are full of danger.

There are good reasons for watching a horror film, even if you're not a loyal horror fan. If you make it through the film in one piece, you'll probably experience a strong sense of mastery, a sense that you were able to make it through an appalling experience. Anyway, watching a horror film makes you better at handling your own fear, and who knows when that will become critically relevant?

46. The alien finds the audience in the movie theatre clearly in a state of
explanation: 【解析】C）第2段第2句提到，在现实世界中，这种斗争与施加在女性身上的潜在压力背道而驰，要求女性符合不切实际的美丽标准。C)项是该句的同义转述，故为答案。

context: Questions 46 to 50 are based on the following passage.
question: Why is horror gratifying to many people?
options: A) It reminds them of an ancient set of biological defence mechanisms.
B) It triggers their imagination to travel into dangerous virtual worlds.
C) It allows them to learn what fear feels like and how to tackle it.
D) It activates their evolved fear system and their unique fantasy.
passage:
Imagine you're an alien sent to Earth to document the behaviour of the mammals inhabiting the planet. You stumble into a movie theatre that's showing the latest Hollywood horror film.

Several dozen humans are gathered together in a dark, undecorated room. They're all staring at a rectangular area on which patterns of light change rapidly.

They are clearly in a state of high arousal. Their heart rate is elevated, they occasionally glance around nervously, and they sometimes jump collectively in their seats, and emit high-pitched warning calls.

Eventually, the lights come up and the rectangular screen goes black. The humans stand up and leave the room, chatting and laughing, and showing signs of pleasure.

Why do these humans voluntarily expose themselves to what appears to be a deeply unpleasant experience? And why do they react so strongly to those patterns of light on a screen?

I am fascinated with the paradox of horror—the strange fact that many people seek out scary entertainment.

I think the answer to the puzzle lies in human nature.

My research suggests that we humans evolved to find pleasure in situations that allow us to experience negative emotions in a safe context. You can see these elements of horror in children's games. Take hide-and-seek for example, which is a simulation of a predator-prey interaction. The kid hides and the adult pretends to be a predator, searching for the child while howling like a dangerous beast.

This simulation gives the child crucial information about how to avoid becoming prey, and children tend to find that kind of activity deeply satisfying, presumably because it gives them a safe experience of a potentially catastrophic scenario.

They find it pleasurable, and pleasure is evolution's way of motivating us toward adaptive behaviour.

Horror is pleasurable to many people because it lets us play with negative emotions and develop coping strategies. We learn what it feels like to be truly afraid, and we learn how to handle negative emotion.

How, then, does horror work? My research suggests that horror works by exploiting an ancient set of biological defence mechanisms—an evolved fear system, which we share with other

animals. But humans are uniquely imaginative, and we use our evolved imagination to travel into virtual worlds that are full of danger.

There are good reasons for watching a horror film, even if you're not a loyal horror fan. If you make it through the film in one piece, you'll probably experience a strong sense of mastery, a sense that you were able to make it through an appalling experience. Anyway, watching a horror film makes you better at handling your own fear, and who knows when that will become critically relevant?

46. The alien finds the audience in the movie theatre clearly in a state of
explanation: 【解析】A）第4段第2句提到，然而如今，“Instagram脸”已成为新的美丽标准。A)项是该句的同义转述，故为答案。

explanation: 【解析】C）由第7段最后一句可知，过度使用滤镜的一个重要后果是，人们开始倾向于把自己当作一个被观察和评价的对象，就像别人从旁边观察和评判一样。C)项是该句的同义转述，故为答案。

context: Questions 51 to 55 are based on the following passage.
question: What do we learn about beauty in the digital arena?
options: A) It dictates the taste of digital media.
B) It has been in the making for years.
C) It has ushered in a new awakening.
D) It is defined by social media.
passage:
An awakening has been taking place in the physical world against the beauty model that has been dictated to us for years. But in the digital arena, social media determines what is considered beautiful.

The two opposing struggles are taking place in parallel. In the physical world, the struggle goes against the underlying pressure exerted on women to conform to an unrealistic beauty ideal. As part of the struggle, various media outlets have presented women whose bodies don't correspond to the so-called ideal. All those women who had previously been excluded from the covers of magazines, television series and the public agenda, have become "legitimate". At the same time, a group of influencers have begun to upload to social media photos of themselves without makeup, and even photos in which they highlight supposed flaws.

Technology has reshaped our beauty ideal and is doing a great job communicating that message to the masses. One of the bizarre legacies of the past decade is the popularity of the "cyborg look", which illustrates what Americans will look like in 2050.

The cyborg look spread rapidly. Today, however, the Instagram face has become the new beauty ideal.

The internalization of accepted beauty norms is much more effective when there is active involvement in the learning process. The active involvement of users is reflected in the gamified (游戏化的) interaction offered by the social media platforms—the ability to like, write a comment, compare, share.

Once the desired beauty ideal has been internalized, users are given tools or features to change

their appearance to suit the accepted beauty ideal such as editing the image, choosing the ideal filter, the right background.

A survey conducted in the United States revealed that more than

filter the images before posting them. And you will not be surprised to hear that the majority of them are women. One of the significant consequences of obsessive filtering is the emerging tendency to treat oneself as an object to be observed and valued, in the same way another person observes and judges from the side.

The effect of the filters is already far beyond amiable amusement. The filters and the entire game played on the networks affect the mental health of the users. According to a study, apps like Instagram, Snapchat and FaceTune allow users to achieve a level of perfection that was previously only observed in beauty magazines.

Even though humanity has always cherished beauty, in the last decade our obsession with looks has reached an unprecedented peak. The time spent on social media creates an urge to achieve an impossible beauty ideal so powerful that the only thing that can fix it is not cosmetic intervention, but mental health care.
explanation: 【解析】B）第9段第2句提到，花在社交媒体上的时间让我们产生了实现不可能实现的美丽标准的冲动，这种冲动如此强烈以至于唯一能解决这个问题的方法不是美容干预，而是心理健康护理。由此可知，应引入心理干预来缓解它，B)项是该句的同义转述，故为答案。

explanation: 【解析】C）对话中男士提到他的家庭情况现在越来越复杂，坚持学习艺术史可能不是一个正确的选择，而后女士问男士为什么要放弃艺术史的学习，男士回答说他父亲目前的经济状况让他意识到，经济上更可行的就业机会不一定是坏事。艺术史滋养了他的灵魂，但无法让他吃饱饭。由此可知，男士放弃学习艺术史是因为他认为这个专业不能让他维持生计。故答案为C)。

D) An increasing number of women filtering their images before uploading them.

explanation: 【解析】B）对话中男士说，现在时间非常宝贵。他的社交生活不复存在，他的学习也几乎跟不上，而他的毕业论文到今年年底就要交了。男士问女士：“我有这么多事情要做，什么时候才能抽出时间来研究工作呢？”由此可知，男士现在真正缺少的是时间。故答案为B)。

context: 段落翻译
question: 北斗(Beidou)卫星导航系统的成功研制是中国自改革开放以来取得的一项重大科技成就。研发人员经过不懈努力，攻克了一系列技术难题，北斗系统最终实现了全球覆盖和高精度定位，使中国成为世界上少数几个独立拥有全球卫星导航系统的国家之一。北斗系统已广泛应用于交通运输、灾害救援、天气预报、公共安全等诸多领域。北斗系统现在已经在国际上得到广泛认可，开始为越来越多的国家和地区提供优质服务。
explanation: 【解析】D）对话开头男士就询问女士是否决定好了期末的商业项目。接下来全文都是他们各自描述了自己期末的商业项目，并对对方的项目表了态。故答案为D)。
